questions.question: Which treaty ended World War II in the Pacific?
questions.explanation: Japan formally surrendered on September 2, 1945, aboard the USS Missouri in Tokyo Bay, officially ending World War II. The formal treaty — the Treaty of San Francisco — was signed in 1951. V-J Day (Victory over Japan Day) is celebrated on August 15 in many countries.
questions.memory_hook: USS Missouri = where Japan surrendered. The battleship became the signing table.

questions.question: The 'Harlem Renaissance' was a cultural movement centered in which U.S. city?
questions.explanation: The Harlem Renaissance was a cultural, social, and artistic explosion centered in Harlem, New York, during the 1920s and 1930s. It produced groundbreaking work in literature (Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston), music (jazz, blues), visual arts, and theater, establishing African American culture as a major force in American life.
questions.memory_hook: Harlem = New York. The Renaissance was the rebirth of Black American art.

questions.question: At the 2009 World Championships in Berlin, Usain Bolt set a world record of 9.58 seconds in which track and field event?
questions.explanation: Usain Bolt's 100m world record of 9.58 seconds, set in Berlin in 2009, still stands today. He averaged approximately 37.6 km/h (23.4 mph) over the distance. Bolt also holds the 200m world record at 19.19 seconds.
questions.memory_hook: 9.58 seconds, Berlin 2009 = Bolt's 100m world record. Still unbeaten.

questions.question: Which country invented table tennis?
questions.explanation: Table tennis was invented in England in the 1880s as an after-dinner drawing room game among the upper class. It was originally called 'ping pong,' based on the sound of the ball. China has dominated the sport internationally since the 1960s, winning more Olympic gold medals than any other nation.
questions.memory_hook: England invented ping pong — China perfected it.

questions.question: In which sport do athletes compete in the 'halfpipe'?
questions.explanation: The halfpipe is a feature used in both skateboarding and snowboarding competitions. Athletes ride up and down the curved walls of a U-shaped structure performing aerial tricks. Halfpipe events are part of the Winter Olympics (snowboarding since 1998) and Summer Olympics (skateboarding since 2020).
questions.memory_hook: Halfpipe: literally half a pipe, U-shaped, and terrifyingly vertical.

questions.question: What band recorded 'Smells Like Teen Spirit'?
questions.explanation: 'Smells Like Teen Spirit' was released by Nirvana in September 1991 on their album 'Nevermind.' It is widely credited with bringing grunge and alternative rock to mainstream audiences. The song's name came from a deodorant called Teen Spirit that Kurt Cobain's then-girlfriend wore.
questions.memory_hook: Nirvana's 'Smells Like Teen Spirit' — named after a deodorant. Changed rock music anyway.

questions.question: Who starred in 'Black Panther' as T'Challa?
questions.explanation: Chadwick Boseman played T'Challa/Black Panther in four MCU films, most notably the 2018 film Black Panther, which grossed $1.3 billion worldwide. Boseman died of colon cancer in August 2020, having privately battled the illness for four years while continuing to work. His death shocked the world.
questions.memory_hook: Boseman IS Wakanda. He played T'Challa while fighting cancer — unseen, undefeated.

questions.question: Which TV detective's signature move was to pause while leaving a suspect's room, turn back, and say 'Just one more thing...' before delivering his key insight?
questions.explanation: 'Just one more thing...' is the signature phrase of Lieutenant Columbo from the TV series Columbo, played by Peter Falk. The seemingly disheveled detective would pretend to leave, then turn back with one devastating final question that usually cracked the case.
questions.memory_hook: Columbo's 'Just one more thing' — the question that always caught the killer.

questions.question: The ancient city of Constantinople is now known by what name?
questions.explanation: Constantinople, the capital of the Byzantine Empire, was renamed Istanbul after the Ottoman conquest in 1453. The name Istanbul derives from a Greek phrase meaning 'to the city' or 'in the city.' It remains Turkey's largest city and cultural center, though Ankara is the current capital.
questions.memory_hook: Constantinople became Istanbul — even The Four Tops sang about the change.

questions.question: What was the name of the British spy organization featured in James Bond films?
questions.explanation: MI6 (Military Intelligence, Section 6), officially known as the Secret Intelligence Service (SIS), is the British foreign intelligence agency. James Bond works for MI6 under the director known as 'M.' The real MI6 headquarters is in Vauxhall, London — depicted in several Bond films.
questions.memory_hook: MI6 = James Bond's employer. The real headquarters overlooks the Thames.

questions.question: Which country contains the most of the Amazon rainforest?
questions.explanation: Approximately 60% of the Amazon rainforest lies within Brazil. The remaining 40% is spread across eight other countries: Peru, Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Bolivia, Guyana, Suriname, and French Guiana. The Amazon produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and holds 10% of all species on Earth.
questions.memory_hook: Amazon = mostly Brazil. 60% in one country. The forest is so large it creates its own weather — the trees release enough water vapor to generate their own rain system.

questions.question: What does the Richter scale measure?
questions.explanation: The Richter scale, developed by Charles Richter in 1935, measures the magnitude (energy released) of earthquakes. It is logarithmic — each whole number increase represents about 31.6 times more energy. A magnitude 7.0 quake releases 31.6 times more energy than a 6.0, and about 1,000 times more than a 5.0.
questions.memory_hook: Richter scale = earthquake strength. Logarithmic — each step is 31 times more powerful. A magnitude 8 isn't twice as strong as a 4; it's millions of times stronger.
